title: When Do Path-Dependent Setup Costs Matter in Sequential Causal Design?
description: This project is a characterization study of sequential causal experimental design under path-dependent setup costs. Instead of proposing a new general planner, it studies when switching costs provably can or cannot change the budget-optimal intervention policy for skeleton-conditional orientation learning, and validates those crossover regimes empirically with exact-posterior audits on small graphs.
motivation: Recent papers already cover additive-cost intervention design, budgeted Bayesian causal design, adaptive online causal discovery, and non-myopic sequential causal BED. The remaining publishable gap is narrower: determine whether family-switching setup costs materially change finite-budget causal experiment schedules, and separate that effect from posterior approximation error, weak baselines, and skeleton-estimation error.
proposed_approach: Estimate an initial CPDAG from observational data, maintain a posterior over CPDAG-consistent linear-Gaussian DAGs, and evaluate short intervention sequences under a fixed-budget terminal orientation-entropy objective rather than an information-to-cost ratio. The main contribution is a structural characterization showing that switching costs matter only when they alter feasibility or when the additive-value gap between policies is small relative to their switch-count gap, plus an empirical study comparing additive-cost planning, switching-aware receding-horizon planning, and a stronger transition-aware finite-horizon tree-search baseline on exact-posterior small-graph settings.

related_work.title: Cost-Optimal Learning of Causal Graphs
related_work.how_it_differs: Studies additive intervention costs for identifying causal graphs, not path-dependent switching costs in finite-sample sequential planning.

related_work.title: Budgeted Experiment Design for Causal Structure Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Uses a budgeted causal design formulation, but costs are additive and the work does not analyze transition-dependent setup costs.

related_work.title: Experimental Design for Cost-Aware Learning of Causal Graphs
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides additive cost-aware intervention design with approximation guarantees, but not sequential transition-cost characterization.

related_work.title: ABCD-Strategy: Budgeted Experimental Design for Targeted Causal Structure Discovery
related_work.how_it_differs: Optimizes Bayesian causal design under explicit budget constraints, but the budget model is not path-dependent.

related_work.title: Adaptive Online Experimental Design for Causal Discovery
related_work.how_it_differs: Studies adaptive online causal discovery with sample-complexity analysis, but not family-switching setup costs.

related_work.title: Sample Efficient Bayesian Learning of Causal Graphs from Interventions
related_work.how_it_differs: Maintains a posterior over graphs under interventions, but does not analyze transition-dependent costs or budgeted policy crossover.

related_work.title: Goal-Oriented Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design for Causal Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Already provides non-myopic sequential causal BED, so this proposal is not claiming a better general sequential planner; it instead isolates the specific effect of path-dependent switching costs under a fixed budget.

related_work.title: Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design with Variable Cost Structure
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that non-uniform sequential cost models matter outside causal discovery, motivating the cost formulation but not solving causal graph orientation.

hypothesis: Switching costs affect the budget-optimal sequential causal intervention policy only when they change policy feasibility or when the additive-value advantage of a higher-switch policy is small relative to its switch-count gap. In those regimes, a switching-aware planner should outperform additive-cost baselines on fixed-budget terminal orientation entropy, and that crossover should persist under exact posterior inference on small graphs.

confirm: The proposed structural condition identifies broad invariance regions where switching costs do not change the preferred policy and crossover regions where they do.

The switching-aware horizon-2 planner improves fixed-budget terminal orientation entropy and area-under-entropy-versus-budget over the matched additive-cost planner in medium and high switching-cost regimes.
The main crossover conclusions survive in the d=8 exact-posterior comparison and are not removed by the stronger transition-aware finite-horizon tree-search baseline.
Results are qualitatively unchanged when moving from the previous ratio metric to budget-aligned objectives.
The learned-CPDAG setting still shows a measurable switching-cost effect, even if weaker than the oracle-CPDAG setting.
The full evaluation fits the 2-core CPU budget.
refute: Exact-posterior or exact-search comparisons eliminate the switching-cost effect.
The result appears only under the ratio metric and disappears under fixed-budget terminal objectives.
Posterior approximation error or skeleton-estimation error dominates the outcome so strongly that switching costs do not have a stable practical effect.
Benefits appear only in one highly stylized intervention-family configuration.
The stronger baseline consistently dominates the switching-aware receding-horizon planner, leaving no useful characterization beyond brute-force search.
